data.title: Boardwalk REIT: Canadian Residential Real Estate At A 35% Discount
data.description:
Introduction

Some of the Canadian residential REITs have gotten increasingly attractive in the past few months. Boardwalk REIT (BEI.UN:CA) (OTCPK:BOWFF) is one of those residential REITs, as despite its strong performance, the share price is currently trading approximately 15% lower than the share price when my previous article was published this summer.

A Strong AFFO Makes the Stock Even More Attractive Than This Summer

While a REIT should never be judged based on its net profit, it's usually the starting point of the FFO calculation, and that’s also the case at Boardwalk REIT. As you can see below, the REIT reported a net profit of C$37.6 million, and after adding back some non-cash items like the fair value decrease in the portfolio and after adding back the distributions on the Class B Units, the FFO came in at almost $66 million, which represents an increase of almost 10% compared to the FFO result in Q3 2024.

On a per-share basis, the improvement is even more visible. The FFO per share came in at C$1.23, which represents an 11% increase. That’s due to the lower total share count, which decreased by approximately 1% to 53.5 million units in the third quarter of 2025.

One of the main reasons why I like Canadian REITs is the fact that their maintenance capex is included in the AFFO calculation. As you can see below, the REIT spent approximately C$8.6 million of the FFO on maintenance capex, and this means the AFFO was C$57.4 million, or C$1.07 per share.

Looking at the 9M 2025 performance, the total AFFO came in at just under C$159 million, representing C$2.97 per share.

That’s a pretty strong Q3 (and subsequently 9M) performance, and this allowed Boardwalk to once again increase its full-year guidance. Whereas the REIT generated just C$3.56 per share in AFFO in 2024, we will now very likely see a 10%+ improvement. As you can see below, Boardwalk initially aimed for an AFFO of C$3.62-3.92 per share but hiked this to C$3.85-4.00 per share after reporting on its Q2 results.

And now, after a strong Q3 result, Boardwalk has further increased its full-year AFFO guidance. The midpoint of C$3.985 per share now represents a 12% increase compared to the AFFO per share in FY 2024 and another 1.5% increase compared to the full-year guidance after the Q2 results were published.

This also means the current distribution rhythm of C$1.62 per share per year (payable in monthly installments) represents a payout ratio of just over 40% of the anticipated AFFO this year. At the current share price, with the stock trading at just over 16 times AFFO and 14 times FFO, Boardwalk’s management is prioritizing stock buybacks. Boardwalk has spent C$31 million on stock buybacks in the first nine months of the year and has spent an additional C$6 million on buying back its own stock after the end of the quarter. This will further boost the per-share performance heading into 2026.

It also means Boardwalk is buying back these units at an attractive discount to the NAV. As you can see below, the total Net Asset Value came in at C$5.23 billion, representing just under C$98/share.

And considering the portfolio is valued at an average capitalization rate of 5.12% (with cap rates ranging from 4.57% in Ontario to 5.68% in Saskatchewan), the NAV/share is quite realistic. The sensitivity analysis below also indicates that for a 25 bps increase in the cap rate, the fair value of the assets decreases by C$378 million.

So even if I were to use a required cap rate of 5.75% in combination with a 1% NOI increase, the net impact on the NAV/share would be less than C$15, as the NAV/share would still come in above C$80 per unit.

Investment Thesis

While I didn’t immediately pull the trigger on initiating a long position in Boardwalk REIT after my previous article was published, I am now ready to do so. I have plenty of Canadian dollars burning a hole in my pocket after selling certain positions into the mining rally in the past few months, and rather than converting those dollars back into euros, I am looking to redeploy my excess amount of Canadian dollars.

Boardwalk REIT fits the bill. The stock is trading at just 16 times the AFFO, with additional AFFO increases to be expected (I now expect C$4.05 and C$4.15 in 2026 and 2027 AFFO per share, respectively), and I think the REIT’s plan and ability to buy back its own shares is a good and value-enhancing move. As the stock is trading at a 35% discount to its NAV, buying back its own shares likely is the best investment Boardwalk can make.

I currently have no position in Boardwalk REIT, but I may initiate a long position in the next few days or weeks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Zhihu: Why It Could Be A Good Time To Get In
data.description:
Zhihu Inc (ZH), a provider of online content in China, spent most of 2025 up substantially for the year, but it is now down slightly for the year with a month to go after losing all gains in the last three months. However, while the selloff in the stock is disconcerting to see, there are a number of reasons to consider long ZH, which includes the possibility of a rebound in the stock that may not be far away if, for instance, the charts are anything to go by.

Why the selloff may be close to over

A previous article from December 2024 written by myself mentioned how ZH had not fared all that well in recent years. The stock was trading far below what it went for shortly after its IPO in 2021, but an argument could be made that with the vast reduction in valuations, ZH was worth taking into consideration, especially if one were to assume the ongoing improvement in the top and the bottom line would continue. In the end, I chose to rate ZH a buy in the article.

The chart above shows how for much of the past 12 months that have passed long ZH was a good move, and at times a great move with strong gains in the stock, especially if we ignore the March-April selloff that can be attributed to the turmoil as a result of the global dispute around tariffs. However, in the past few months, longs have been put to the test with the stock losing all its gains to put ZH down slightly for the year after closing at $3.52 per ADS on December 2, which is just $0.02 below $3.54, or where ZH closed out 2024.

As recently as September, ZH was still comfortably up with a high of $5.55 that month, which gave ZH a gain of 57% YTD, but the stock’s decline in the past several months has wiped out all gains. The decline continued after ZH released its most recent report on November 25, which saw the stock fall to as low as $3.48 in its aftermath.

Why now could be a good time to get in on ZH

However, while the decline in the stock is discouraging, there is also reason to think the decline has likely played out for the most part, if not entirely. First, the stock is now oversold with, for instance, an RSI reading in the mid twenties, after the large decline of the past several months. This alone could power a bounce in the stock, if only because the stock has dropped by so much in a relatively short amount of time.

Furthermore, the stock has reached the $3.20-3.60 price region. The significance of this is that the stock has a history of halting a decline in this region and turning it around to head higher. The chart above shows several instances of the stock heading down, only to turn north in or close to the $3.20-3.60 region. If this patterns holds, ZH is due for another rebound in the stock.

Long ZH is worth a look at current valuations

In addition, the flip side of the decline in the price of the stock is that getting in on ZH is easier due to lower valuations. For instance, the balance sheet is not only in good shape, but one could even argue ZH is undervalued at current valuations with the present state of the balance sheet. According to the most recent or Q3 FY2025 report, ZH has cash and cash equivalents totaling $383.48M, short-term investments totaling $146.77M and term deposits totaling $83.91M on the balance sheet, or a combined $614.17M.

This is substantially more than what ZH is valued at as a company with a market cap of $316M, or almost half the cash ZH has access to, or what can be quickly converted to cash. There is $21.2M of total debt that needs mentioning, but there is also another $25.73M of long-term investments and another $29.5M of non-current term deposits on the balance sheet.

If we add everything up, ZH has a book value of $578.94M with total assets of $772.78M and total liabilities of $193.84M, which converts to a book value of $6.45 per ADS since the total number of ADS/ADRs is 89,692K. This means ZH is currently available at much less than book value with a price-to-book of 0.54x and the stock last closing at $3.52 per ADS. An argument can thus be made that ZH is currently undervalued based on the balance sheet.

What is weighing on ZH?

However, while the Q3 FY2025 report did not leave the bulls empty-handed, there is a reason why the stock fell after the release of the Q3 FY2025 report. One of the main reasons why the article from last year was bullish was because even though ZH was likely to record its fourth consecutive annual loss in FY2024, there was reason to believe FY2025 had a good shot at turning a profit, or at least break even, with ZH making good progress in terms of improving the bottom line.

In fact, there was a clear progression in terms of quarterly earnings, which, assuming it continued, put ZH on the path towards getting out of the red to turn a profit, the first in years. FY2025 is still likely to end the year with its first profit after four years of losses, but what may appeared to have been a one-way road for the bottom line has become a two-way road. This is because the earnings picture has become more hazy with ZH doing well in some quarters and not so well in other quarters.

This is what happened in the Q3 FY2025 report with earnings incurring a large sequential decline QoQ to record a loss after a solid profit in the preceding quarter. ZH reported a non-GAAP net loss of RMB21M or $2.95M in Q3 FY2025, which is not only worse than in Q2 FY2025, but also compared to one year ago in Q3 FY2024, unlike past quarters where the number would consistently show improvement YoY.

In terms of GAAP, ZH incurred a net loss of RMB46.74M or $6.57M in Q3 FY2025, much worse than the net loss of RMB8.98M in Q3 FY2024 or the net profit of RMB72.48M in Q2 FY2025. The main difference between the GAAP and non-GAAP number is the latter’s exclusion of share-based compensation expense to the tune of RMB22.85M. On a per share basis, ZH lost RMB0.58 or $0.08 per ADS.

The decrease in earnings was primarily driven by a decrease in revenue from RMB845M in Q3 FY2024 to RMB658.9M or $92.6M in Q3 FY2025. Nonetheless, earnings have improved in 2025. GAAP net income totaled $0.15 per ADS TTM, despite the loss in Q3 FY2025. It also continues the gradual improvement achieved by ZH seen in recent years.

ZH posted net losses of $2.89, $2.26, $1.19 and $0.26 in FY2021, FY2022, FY2023 and FY2024, respectively, all per ADS. Not only is the trend positive, but the current net profit of $0.15 per ADS builds on the improvement, the Q3 FY2025 results notwithstanding. ZH would need to post more losses to break the existing pattern, but this does not necessarily have to happen.

It’s worth mentioning that net income at ZH is boosted by non-operating income like interest and other investment income due to the cash reserves ZH holds, which can fluctuate from quarter to quarter. In terms of, for instance, operating income, which leaves the aforementioned out, ZH posted a non-GAAP operating loss of RMB73.47M in Q3 FY2025, which is an improvement versus the operating loss of RMB87.81M Q3 FY2024, but still somewhat worse than the loss of RMB71.55M in Q2 FY2025.

In other words, ZH did better from an operational standpoint in Q3 FY2025 than the headline numbers would lead you to believe. There is no guidance from ZH to work with, but ZH deserves the benefit of the doubt that it will maintain the gradual improvement seen in recent years. Q3 FY2025 is one quarter and one quarter does not make a trend. The current trend still points to further improvement in earnings, especially on an annual basis, although this may fluctuate from quarter to quarter.

The role of AI remains in flux

ZH is essentially a content provider where users, including those who pay a subscription to ZH, can access such things as articles written by professionals in the field. However, with the rise of generative AI, content providers like ZH could in theory see a decline in monthly active users over the long run as people turn to AI.

On the other hand, it is worth mentioning that ZH is benefiting from AI in the sense that it is used to provide data to train large language models, especially since its articles are considered to be of higher quality than most other content, on top of being more trustworthy. ZH itself has also started to apply AI internally.

It’s probably too early to conclude whether AI is a net positive or a net negative for ZH, or a mixture of both. It’s also possible for ZH to stand out since it relies on humans to generate content, which could become less common over time. This could make ZH something that the public, or at least a portion of it, can value to the point that a paid subscription is well worth it. This could affect how subscriptions hold up over the long term.

Investor takeaways

ZH has definitely not done well recently as far as the stock’s performance is concerned. The stock has fallen for almost all of the last three months, which has reduced the price of the stock by 36.6% since the September high. It does not help that the most recent quarterly report from ZH included a decline in both revenue and net income on November 25, unlike the overall improvement in results that had come to characterize recent quarterly results.

However, there is nonetheless an argument to be made in favor of long ZH. ZH has gradually improved profitability, on a 12-months basis, and this has not really changed, in spite of the Q3 FY2025 report. ZH needs to improve the income statement in upcoming quarters, but it also deserves the benefit of the doubt that it can get it done, especially since that is exactly what it did in recent history. FY2025 has a quarter to go, but FY2025 is likely to continue with what the previous four did, which is to show an improvement in the bottom line.

In addition, the balance sheet is such that ZH has plenty of room to work on the income statement. ZH is actually valued at less than net cash, including what can be quickly converted to cash, on the books. ZH may not yet deserve to trade close to book value because some cash might get consumed due to free cash flow, which is still slightly in the red, but the present valuation of $3.52 per ADS looks too low to the point ZH is arguably undervalued. Somewhere between the current stock price and book value looks more fair, which suggests a stock price of around $5.00 per ADS, or more than 40% above where ZH currently trades at.

The charts also suggest that the stock could be close to reversing the decline in the stock. The selloff has already lasted several months and the stock is well oversold. The stock price is also close to a region where it has a history of bottoming and moving higher and while this does not guarantee a rebound in the stock, it does suggest that now is as good a time as any to be placing bullish bets, especially when accounting for the other points raised earlier.

I am therefore bullish on ZH. ZH needs to work on the income statement, but ZH deserves the opportunity to prove it can do it with its recent track record. Add the cash on the balance sheet, the position of the stock in the charts and there is enough to think that long ZH has what it takes to become a winning play. The decline of the past few months will then have turned out to be nothing more than an opportunity to get in on a stock like ZH at a bargain.

data.title: Why I Think Scotiabank's 30% Rally Isn't Done Yet
data.description:
Thesis

The Bank of Nova Scotia (NYSE:BNS), better known as Scotiabank, is a Canadian multinational that began life in 1832 in Halifax and now operates out of Toronto, and as you’d expect from a bank with that sort of reach, it offers a broad sweep of financial services around the world, from personal and commercial banking to wealth and investment management.

By the looks of it, BNS’s stock has been grinding upward since April, moving in a slow, orderly channel that’s delivered investors a solid 30% return year-to-date.

After going through the Q4 earnings released this week, my research suggests that BNS is in the middle of a genuine step up in quality: EPS, ROE, and efficiency are all moving in the right direction, wealth and capital-markets are carrying more of the load, and the Canadian retail bank is gradually mending its margins and its credit picture.

Bank of Nova Scotia: Financial Highlights and Strategic Progress

I’m starting with the simple fact that BNS has just posted an earnings beat, even as the broader economy wheezes along. North America is juggling shifting tariffs, and Canada’s manufacturing PMI has been lingering below 50 while trade tensions with the U.S. rumble on. Yet somehow, in the middle of all that, BNS still managed to turn in solid results.

In that setting, BNS reported Q4 adjusted earnings per share EPS of $1.93, which is up 23% from last year, on revenue of $9.8 billion, which grew about 15%, and for the full year, EPS came in at $7.09, up 10%. Management focused heavily on tightening operations and improving the balance sheet. ROE for Q4 was 12.5%, up 190 basis points. The efficiency ratio improved to 54.3%, better by 180 basis points. For the year, revenue grew 12% while expenses were up 9%, giving the bank 3% positive operating leverage.

Net interest income rose 13% in the quarter, driven by a 25-basis-point increase in the bank’s overall net interest margin compared to last year, an improvement that came from lower funding costs and a better mix of deposits. Noninterest income was strong too, up 16% on the back of higher wealth-management fees, stronger underwriting and advisory revenue, and a lift from the KeyCorp stake.

On the other hand, the company’s expenses rose 11% compared to last year, mainly because it spent more on employees and technology. Credit costs also stayed high, with about $1.1 billion set aside for loan losses in the fourth quarter. That works out to a 58-basis-point PCL ratio, which kept the full-year PCL ratio at 62 basis points. By the end of the year, the allowance for credit losses totaled 98 basis points of loans, after the company added $630 million to its performing loan reserves expected to cover risks through 2025.

In Canadian Banking, the full year looked worse in hindsight than the picture at the end of Q4. The business earned $3.4 billion for the year, which was down 9%. About $600 million in higher loan-loss provisions and lower profit margins caused by interest rate cuts outweighed the 3% revenue growth and 5% increase in expenses, resulting in negative operating leverage for the year.

In the quarter, the segment earned $942 million, which was up 1% from a year earlier. Pretax, pre-provision profit rose 3%, and the trends in loans and deposits began to reflect the strategic changes management has been trying to make. Average loans were up 2%, helped along by a 4% rise in mortgages and a modest 1% lift in credit cards.

Deposits, meanwhile, slipped by 1%, but that was by design, the bank let its pricier term deposits roll off and shifted its attention to the more ordinary, everyday transaction accounts. It worked: retail savings deposits climbed 7%, day-to-day balances rose 6%, retail term deposits fell 8%, and nonpersonal deposits edged down 2%.

NIM was still 2 basis points lower than a year ago, but it inched up by 1 basis point from the previous quarter, a tiny move, but a meaningful one because it fits neatly with management’s plan for mid–single-digit pretax, pre-provision growth by 2026, helped along by margins improving as a big batch of fixed-rate mortgages gets repriced and deposit spreads strengthen while the deposit mix continues shifting their way.

On top of that, the Canadian team is looking for fee growth to approach double digits next year, coming from insurance, mutual fund distribution, credit cards, and cash-management services. They’re also expecting loan-loss provisions to “normalize.” In practice, that means the Canadian PCL ratio, 43 basis points this year, should ease into the high-30-basis-point range by 2026, as the strain in both mortgages and unsecured lending begins to calm after a rough stretch in the Greater Toronto Area.

Scaling Profits with Less Risk

The star of the year was Global Wealth Management as earnings increased 17% to $1.7 billion for the full year, with the fourth quarter alone contributing $453 million (also up 17%). Assets under management grew 16% to $430 billion and assets under administration rose 13% to almost $800 billion. In Canada, people traded stocks more actively, private-banking loans and deposits both grew double-digits, and mutual-fund fees were strong.

Outside Canada, the bank’s international wealth business had a strong year, with earnings jumping 30% thanks to a 25% increase in assets and better profit margins on interest. Since its 2023 Investor Day (see progress above), the bank has gained 400,000 new primary clients and has gotten much better at converting those clients into actual investors.

A major reason for this improvement is stronger teamwork inside the company. I know that sort of line often gets treated like a tired cliché, but I don’t think it deserves to be softened. A team-focused business culture can genuinely make or break an organization, and it’s worth pointing out when it’s real. As a result, referrals from the regular retail bank to the wealth division reached $8.1 billion (up 20%), and total referrals across the whole bank hit $15 billion (up 18%). Altogether, these gains position the bank’s wealth-management fees to grow steadily through 2026, even if the stock market slows down.

Essentially, the capital-markets and international divisions together show how the bank is managing to make more money without letting the balance sheet swell to unwieldy proportions.

Global Banking & Markets earned $1.9 billion for the year, a 30% increase, with $519 million in the fourth quarter alone, up 50%. Revenue rose 24% thanks to a 43% jump in trading and advisory fees and better lending and deposit margins.

Crucially, actual loans fell 13% while deposits increased, so the higher profits came from using less capital, tightening up pricing, and leaning more on fee-based businesses, keep in mind, half of these earnings now come from the United States, helped by a new cash-management platform that brought in 15,000 corporate clients in 2025.

International Banking earned $2.7 billion, up 1%, with fourth-quarter earnings of $638 million, up 3%. Meanwhile, loans slipped 2%, deposits rose 4%, expenses barely moved, and return on equity edged toward 15% and a recent acquisition in Colombia is already contributing to profits. The combined operations there, by the way, will run under a new holding company called Davivienda Group.

On the risk front, credit losses remain elevated, an impaired PCL ratio of 54 basis points for 2025, thanks to pressure in Chile’s consumer loans and parts of Mexico. Even so, newer loans are holding up much better. The bank closed the year with a solid CET1 capital ratio of 13.2%, despite having bought back 10.8 million shares.

Management expects credit losses to improve in 2026, landing in the high-40s to mid-50s basis-point range. They plan to keep their capital level around 13%, continue buying back shares, and still deliver double-digit earnings growth next year. They also believe that shifting more of the business toward wealth management and high-return capital-markets activities, together with Canada’s new infrastructure initiative, Grow Canada, makes their medium-term goal of earning a return on equity above 14% more achievable than they originally thought.

BNS’s Valuation

When I look at BNS at around $70, most of the data makes it appear undervalued, that is to say, as long as the bank actually delivers on its growth plans. The forward P/E ratio is 12.18, slightly above the sector median of 11.22 but still well below a fair-value benchmark of 15x.

Using that, the stock would be worth only about $76.74 today. And if you apply the long-term expected EPS growth rate, you get a potential stock price of about $104 by 2028 based on consensus forecasts.

That works out to an 11% annual return, boosted by a 4.37% dividend yield (see historical stats above) that’s 53% higher than the industry average.

In the meantime, revenue's growing 12% year-over-year, beating the sector’s 8.45%, and forward EPS is expected to rise 12%, also ahead of the 10.19% median.

All of this goes back to and points to a bank generating real growth from its wealth and capital-markets arms instead of leaning solely on its Canadian retail business.

But valuation isn’t a free ride. The trailing-twelve-month price-to-cash-flow ratio is 22.54, more than double the sector’s 9.68, and it does look expensive once you see how uneven Scotiabank’s real operating cash flow has been.

As you can see, the TTM operating number is actually around –$10.1 billion, after earlier wild swings from big negatives like –$57.9 billion and –$73.8 billion to sharp recoveries such as $11.8 billion in 2024. There’s no outright burn here, but the volatility does point to some execution stumbles in the international operations, where slipping Latin American currencies are dragging on growth and pulling attention away from the core business in Canada.

Final Takeaway

Nevertheless, overall, I’d rate BNS as a Buy. The bank's showing real momentum in how it runs its business: EPS, ROE, profit margins, and its fee-based businesses are all heading in the right direction. Valuation isn’t exactly dirt cheap and cash-flow volatility is a risk, even so, with double-digit expected earnings growth, a 4%+ yield, and credible strategic execution, I believe the risk-reward still leans in our favor.

data.title: Dollar General: A Clean Beat And A Re-Rating In Sight
data.description:
Dollar General Reports Earnings Amid Inflation Fatigue

The health of the consumer is this week's topic, as we see several retailers reporting earnings. We saw that a two-economy scenario is setting up, with higher-income households showing resilience, while the recent U.S. Census Bureau retail data seems to suggest that lower-income earners are reducing their non-discretionary spending. Data on real earnings also play a role: when real wage growth for the bottom deciles of the consumer population lags inflation, discount stores usually perform better. In September, real average hourly earnings for production and nonsupervisory employees (aka, lower-income earners) decreased by a penny MoM to $9.93 (-0.1%). Weekly hours moved up from 33.6 to 33.7 MoM, thus helping these workers earn a bit more on a weekly and monthly timeframe, but we are seeing official data that confirms what we have been hearing this whole year: lower-income consumers are under pressure. This phenomenon is also called inflation fatigue and is often favorable for dollar stores. This is why we are seeing Dollar General and Dollar Tree performing well this year after their share price had literally crumbled after their 2022 peaks.

So, in this environment, retailers such as Dollar Tree (DLTR) and Dollar General (DG) usually thrive as they also benefit from trade-down behaviors from middle-income earners (<$100k). We just dealt with Dollar Tree's strong quarter, and now we can review Dollar General's earnings.

Dollar General: An Overview Of The Company

This is the first time I have covered Dollar General, so let me briefly paint what the company does. It is the largest discount retailer in the U.S. with over 20,500 locations in the country and in Mexico (where the expansion plan began in 2023). In its last Annual Report, the company proudly boasted that since 1990, it has been able to achieve positive same-store sales with the only exception of 2021, which suffered from a tough comparison as in 2020, due to the pandemic, Dollar General's sales skyrocketed.

The retailer's merchandise mix tilts heavily toward consumables, which account for more than 82% of total sales; seasonal items follow (holiday items, toys, gardening supplies, automotive supplies, and similar); home products and apparel are two minor categories.

The company targets the non-urban areas of the country, with over 80% of its stores found in towns of 20k or fewer people. Its supply chain is also a big part of the business, as it helps the company keep its prices low. Dollar General discloses that only 4% of its purchases are directly imported, which means the company is highly protected from tariffs.

When we deal with a discount store such as Dollar General, we have to control two main items of the income statement. Sales will always be a key driver of the overall performance, but then we also need to closely monitor the cost of goods sold and the operating costs. A big part of the equation is solved when these two lines are kept under tight control.

Dollar General's Q3 2025 Earnings Review

In coherence with what we briefly recalled above, Dollar General's Q3 Sales topped consensus estimates. The company reported revenues of $10.65B representing a 4.6% YoY growth and coming in $50M above estimates. Its EPS was $1.28, beating analysts estimates by $0.35. This represents 44% YoY growth, which is rightly receiving credit by the market as DG shares are up almost 7% in pre-market trading. The company was able to open an additional 196 stores, while remodeling over 1,100 through its projects. These strong results made Dollar General revise its FY25 guidance upward, which now targets net sales growth of around 4.8% (prior guidance: 4.55% at midpoint), driven by same-store sales growth of 2.6% (prior guidance: 2.35% at midpoint). While the FY25 EPS consensus forecasted $6.14, Dollar General disclosed that its EPS should be between $6.30 and $6.50.

We also see that interest expense decreased 17.6% YoY to $55.9M and this helped the company's net income grow by almost 44% YoY to $282.7M.

Let's look at Dollar General's gross profit and operating costs.

As a percentage of net sales, gross profit grew to 29.9%, up by 107 bps YoY. Dollar General explained that this growth was fueled by "inventory markups and lower shrink". Dollar General had to increase prices a bit, partly due to tariffs and partly because of persistent inflation across the economy. But the news that shrink is trending down is positive because it reflects improved store and inventory management.

SG&A grew by 20 bps to 25.9% of net sales. Again, we see that Dollar General was able to keep its cost structure under control, thus using its momentum in sales to expand its margins and its profits.

To confirm the smooth waters Dollar General is sailing through, we can look at its merchandise inventories. At cost, they were $6.7B, a $400M decrease YoY (-8.2%). This means Dollar General is seeing volume growth.

Undoubtedly, Dollar General's quarter was a success, and the improved guidance bodes well for the future.

Dollar General's Valuation

Let's then look at its valuation. Are DG shares attractive right now? If we consider the new guidance and today's price surge, the new fwd PE is 18.4x, which is a bit lower than Dollar Tree. When we consider the fwd EV/EBITDA ratio, we see that DG trades around a 13x multiple, while its main competitor is close to 14. So right now, Dollar General cheaper than its peer. It is growing at a slower pace (4.6% vs. 5.25%), but it is also far larger in size. Moreover, Dollar General pays a dividend (2.15% yield), while Dollar Tree doesn't (it buys back shares, though). However, Dollar Tree's dividend grows very slowly and should not be considered a great fit for dividend growth investors. Here, we are in the field of defensive plays with some upside.

Given the current momentum, I think there is a bit of room for the stock to rerate and expand its multiple through this quarter, anticipating strong Q4 sales. Usually, the last quarter of the year is the best one for many retailers, and this is also the case for DG. If DG closes this year with EPS of $6.50, which I think is likely, then we will have to move our 2026 EPS estimate up to around $7.00. This would give us a fwd PE of 16.86, which I think becomes a bit low, especially in the case of a shift in market sentiment that makes investors rotate to value and defensive plays after the AI-driven bull market. With a multiple of 19, we could have a price target of $133, which represents 13% upside. It might not be huge, but it shows that there is some value right now in the stock. Therefore, I rate it as a buy.

data.title: NVDL: Better Safe Than Sorry
data.description:
Thesis

Leveraged ETF are very high risk financial instruments, and the regulators have taken notice:

Dec 3, 2025 (Reuters) - The U.S. securities market regulator has paused the review of proposals for new highly leveraged exchange-traded funds from several fund managers and sought more clarity on the risks tied to the products.

In its warning letters sent to nine ETF providers including Direxion, ProShares and GraniteShares on Tuesday, the Securities and Exchange Commission said some funds have sought to track as much as fives times the performance of the underlying stock.

While leveraged ETFs can work like magic in up-trending markets creating wealth, they do the opposite during downturns, and do it quite fast. Suffice to look at the 2x leveraged Strategy (MSTR) ETF, namely the Defiance Daily Target 2X Long MSTR ETF (MSTX), which is down -84% this year.

In today's article we are going to visit a very popular name and its 2x leveraged ETF, and highlight why investors should take profit and switch to 1x exposures as the cycle turns.

2x Nvidia? There is an ETF for that

The GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF (NVDL) represents a 2x take on the Nvidia (NVDA) stock:

The Fund seeks daily investment results, before fees and expenses, of 2 times (200%) the daily percentage change of the common stock of NVIDIA Corp. There is no guarantee that the Fund will meet its stated objective. The fund should not be expected to provide 2 times the cumulative return of NVDA for periods greater than a day.

The ETF has fared fairly well this year, although the volatility has been substantial:

NVDL is up +25% this year, but had a -60% drawdown in April 2025. The ETF comes with a 98% annualized volatility figure, and that number is not one to understate. The S&P 500 (SPX) has a 19% annualized volatility for example. A high volatility figure means the ETF can move significantly and wipe out gains quite fast.

Also please note the compounding slippage for NVDL. If NVDA is up +33% for the year, one would expect a 2x product to have a +66% figure. However NVDL is up only 25%. This is due to the product feature which replicates the return for one day only, and experiences significant slippage in volatile, choppy markets like 2025.

Leveraged ETFs are only suitable for normalized up-trending markets

While the ETF provider makes it clear that the fund is only suitable for a 2x daily exposure, many investors do not really get behind that statement. The big red flag here to heed is that significant drops in NVDA can completely wipe out a 2x ETF. Suffice to look at MSTX which is down -85% for 2025. The issue with such violent moves is that even a doubling overnight of the underlying stock still leaves 2x leveraged ETF down a lot for the year. We explored that topic in detail here.

2x leveraged ETFs need normal up-trending markets to deliver the best results. Volatile, choppy markets result in underperformance, and a significant down move similar to 2022 can create unmanageable losses. Let us look at what Nvidia did in 2022:

While Nvidia is a 'Magnificent 7' and has fully recovered its 2022 drawdown, during October of that year it was down -60%. If we apply the same drawdown for 2026 for Nvidia, we will get a move lower in the 2x ETF in excess of -90%. That is an almost complete wipe-out, and holders would need a long and slow up-trending market to make that amount back.

It is very hard to come back from a -90% drawdown, even if the underlying stock does eventually recover given the compounding slippage for a 2x leveraged ETF. The best strategy here given the present information is to try to avoid such a scenario at all costs, and move to a 1x direct exposure when the cycle looks like it is turning.

Markets are stretched - this means caution is warranted

While nobody can time the market, overall valuation levels look stretched by traditional metrics:

In the above chart, courtesy of Goldman, the blue squares represent the historic P/E ranges, while the orange dot represents the current P/E figure. As we can see in the U.S. that number is very high above the normal range.

The Nvidia forward P/E as per Seeking Alpha is 39x, much higher than the S&P 500 number, but more in line with growth names. As a reminder Nvidia is the largest weighted stock in the S&P 500 index, with a current weighting of 7.5%. A significant downturn in the index will see a similar and magnified move lower in Nvidia.

Are we saying that Nvidia will collapse and never recover? No. Are we warning investors that a recession can see a very deep drawdown in this stock? Absolutely. Again, the name moved lower by -60% in 2022.

Conclusion

NVDL is a 2x leveraged ETF, aiming to provide the daily 2x return of the Nvidia stock. The name suffers from compounding slippage, and is up only +25% this year versus 33% for Nvidia due to volatile and choppy markets. If we get another drawdown like in 2022 for Nvidia, NVDL is set to lose in excess of 90% of its current value, thus creating significant loses. With markets overpriced by traditional metrics, we are of the opinion investors would do well to move to the stock outright (i.e. a 1x exposure) or just liquidate the entire position.

data.title: The Wind Is At Its Back: Why Cadeler Could Re-Rate Sharply By 2028
data.description:
Recommendation

I recommend a Buy on Cadeler (CDLR) as it supported by strong Q3 2025 results and a visible transition towards free cash flow generation as peak Capex rolls off by 2026. The company is also uniquely positioned to benefit from an impending supply-demand imbalance in offshore wind vessel availability while its full-scope installation capacity provides a structural moat that the market underappreciates. Despite execution risk, CDLR trades at multiples well below its peers, offering good asymmetric upside. Buy.

Q3 2025 Earnings Summary

CDLR has delivered a strong Q3 2025 performance with 9M2025 revenue up by 178% y/y, which is driven by high fleet utilization (92.2% vs 86.5% in 2024), new vessel deployments and also significant termination fees from Ørsted under a Long-Term agreement. EBITDA surged to €322 mil from €71mil while net profit jumped to €232mil. This reflects robust operating leverage and scale benefits from fleet expansion.

9M 2025 Cash flow operations also massively increased more than sixfold to €286mil while Capex soared to €931mil, mainly contributed by final payments on the three newbuilds (Wind Maker, Wind Pace and Wind Ally) (page 2 of 3Q 2025 investor presentation). The company has reaffirmed its full-year guidance of €588 - €628mil in revenue and €381- €421mil in EBITDA. More importantly, CDLR also grew its order backlog to €2.8bn as of November 2025. Thus, these deals should extend visibility well into 2030, underscoring management's strategic execution amid sector headwinds.

Investment Thesis

From my observation, I believe that the market is underappreciating CDLR as there are various drivers that the market is not paying enough attention such as the supply crunch, its "full-scope moat" and its transition to cash flow generative and shareholder returns.

Secular Growth & Supply Crunch

CDLR would be able to benefit from an inflection in supply-demand from 2026 onwards which leads to a sustained high utilization and improving day-rates. This is supported as the offshore wind industry is poised for massive expansion towards the end of the decade as the supply of installation vessels is currently constrained.

In fact, from 2027 onwards, the vessel demand would outstrip supply by 2028-30 and demand for turbine installation would be 2-3x of the available vessels by 2030. Thus, this impending undersupply would serve as a strong tailwind for the company as it ensures that there would be fewer idle gaps and this would also grant higher pricing power to negotiate for higher contract rates or more favorable terms.

Full-Scope Moat

I believe CDLR ability to offer integrated foundation and turbine installation would drive margin expansion and market share gains. To substantiate, the company is one of the first to secure a major full-scope T&I contract for both foundations and turbines (Hornsea 3). This is substantial because traditionally developers would split these scopes between a heavy-vessel contractor and a WTIV contractor which would introduce interface risk and inefficiency.

However, CDLR can handle both scopes and optimize the project schedules such as staggering foundation and turbine installations which potentially reduce total installation time. This is very valuable to clients as each day saved is worth hundreds of thousands in cost. This would also mean higher revenue per project and leading to better margins in the long run.

Notably, the market tends to recognize CDLR as a "Wind Turbine Installation vessel company" and may not fully appreciate it as foundation installer. For example, foundation installation can command higher day rates which would also boost ARPU.

Cash Machine Transformation

CDLR is on the cusp of transition to strong cash flow generation, and shareholder returns which could attract more institutional investors and potentially rerate the stock. The company has been in the heavy investment mode, burning cash for newbuilds such as the heavy capex in 9M2025. This suppresses free cash flow which may deter some investors who like companies with only with positive free cash flow.

2025 represents the year of peak capex. By 2026, CDLR would largely complete its fleet expansion with only two vessels (Wind Ace, Wind Apex) to complete. Thus, it would likely use existing financing and operating cash. Moving to 2027, capex should drop to maintenance levels which is relatively low as vessels have long life and big overhauls which are periodic. Meanwhile, operating cash flow is rising as stated above in 9M 2025. The company is guiding full year 2025 EBITDA of €400mil and OCF of €380mil based on guidance.

From 2026, the company could generate hundreds of millions in FCF annually. For instance, if 2027 EBITDA is €350mil and CAPEX is at €50mil, interest at €50mil and taxes perhaps €20mil. This would bring adjusted FCF to €230mil. With a €1.5bn market cap, that would bring to an implied FCF yield of ~15%. The market hasn't priced in that scenario. Some catalysts could be triggered like positive FCF quarter in 2026 or even a dividend policy announcement should they be flushed with cash in the future.

Bull Case

In my bull scenario, CDLR was able to effectively monetize its industry-leading fleet of next-gen wind turbine and foundation installation vessels. With 92% utilization in Q3 2025 and also with a €2.8bn backlog, I think CDLR has strong revenue visibility through 2027. Assuming there is successful execution for its full scope projects like Hornsea 3 and continued high vessel uptime, revenue could potentially grow at ~20% CAGR from €558mil in 2025 to over €1bn by 2028. With operating leverage and expanded fleet, EBITDA margins can easily reach above 55% and normalized EPS reaching ~$7.8 by 2028. Do note that 2027 EPS estimate is at $7.8. I am already being conservative here and also the EPS estimate grows exponentially. Thus, $7.8 is very much achievable.

Given strong sector tailwinds like the vessel undersupply post-2029 and offshore wind policy support in EU and APAC, CDLR may command a multiple of 6x which supports a 2028e price target of $46.80. This already implies an IRR of >50%.

Bear Case

In my bear case, the offshore wind sector could continue to face delays from auction failures, permitting bottlenecks and inflation in various components' cost. This could lead to lower-than-expected utilization and pricing pressure in a crowded European market. CDLR may also struggle to fill capacity profitably and some backlog might also slip or be renegotiated.

Revenue growth could also stagnate or decline, and EPS may only reach less than what analyst estimate. I expect in a bear case, 2028e EPS may just be $5.0 which is near 2026e estimate. In this case, the market might just assign a lower P/E multiple at 3x and that would bring my target price to $15.

In conclusion, the bull-bear case is positively skewed, and this justifies a Buy as the investment case hinges on the company turning to profitability.

Valuation

CDLR appears attractive relative to its industrial sector peers, particularly on earnings and cash flow metrics. Its TTM GAAP P/E ratio stands at just 4.9x versus its sector median of 24.2x, which is a massive 79% discount. Moreover, both the forward EV/EBITDA (6.6x) and forward EV/EBIT (9.4x) is trading at substantial discount of 46% and 45% respectively.

However, its revenue multiples are not as attractive as EV/Sales is at 4.6x which is more than double the sector median of 2.2x. This reflects that market recognize CDLR's long-term revenue certainty from fixed contracts. The company is also taking delivery of multiple next-generation vessels, but they only began contributing revenue in 2026-2027. Thus, this explains the elevated sales multiple. Price to book multiple also trades at a discount at 0.9x. Overall, the company appears to be trading at a discount on its earnings-based metrics and once the new orders start to recognize revenue, the sales multiple would compress.

Risk

CDLR face several key risks that could impair its earnings power and valuation. The most immediate is offshore wind projects delay particular in Europe and US where permitting challenges and auction cancellations have already caused developers to defer projects to 2028-2029. This also cause a potential utilization dip for CDLR's newly delivered fleet especially in the mid-cycle period, pressuring day rates and margins.

In addition, if utilization weakens, CDLR may struggle to maintain return on capital which also runs risks to our thesis. Finally, competition from emerging Asia and consolidation in the offshore wind EPC space could also tighten pricing and erode its current cost advantage.

Conclusion

In conclusion, CDLR offers a compelling upside opportunity which is driven by operational momentum and structural tailwinds with discounted valuation. While there are still some risks, the company's growing backlog, fleet scale and transition to cash generative are drivers to look out for. Buy.

data.title: Ralliant: Some Operating Momentum And Potential For This Spin-Off
data.description:
Shares of Ralliant (RAL) have become a publicly traded business since the summer after Fortive (FTV) decided to spin off its precision technologies business. Shares fell out of the gate, which is not too uncommon for spin-offs, but these have recovered to the separation price half a year later.

This comes as the business has seen some signs of operating momentum after posting sales declines in recent quarters.

Being well positioned, with potential margins also to be delivered upon, Ralliant looks like an interesting business that is trading at a reasonable valuation here. While this is the case, real execution needs to be delivered upon to drive appeal. Right now, I feel no need to get involved just yet but will keep a close eye on the business going forward to 2026.

Create Confidence Through Precision Technologies

Ralliant is a developer and manufacturer of instruments as well as sensors & safety systems, which are used for applications that require accuracy, reliability, and safety.

In general, the business provides mission-critical systems in demanding environments. In total, the business employs over 7,000 workers across more than 20 sites, which serve 90,000 customers. With flagship brands like Tektronix, Qualitrol, Gems Setra, and more, the business generates over $2 billion in annual sales, these being profitable sales with EBITDA margins seen in the low 20s.

The sensor & safety systems segment makes up about 60% of this revenue base, serving clients in general industrial, defense & space, utilities, and other applications. The test & measurement segment is comprised of semiconductors, electronics, and communication markets, these generally being more capex-related activities.

The company targets through-the-cycle revenue growth of 3-5%, growing margins, and thereby a balanced capital allocation strategy focused on creating shareholder value.

Trading Flat

Shares of Ralliant traded around the $50 mark by the end of June, when the spin-off was completed. Thereafter, shares quickly moved down to the lower forties as the first quarterly earnings report was released in August. Near the middle of the month, the company reported its second quarter results for the year; that was for the quarter that ended until the date of the effective separation.

Second quarter sales fell 6% to $503 million, as GAAP operating profits were cut nearly in half to $95 million, as GAAP earnings of $48 million yielded GAAP earnings of $0.42 per share based on a share tally of 112 million shares. Adjusted for some items, mostly relating to amortization charges, adjusted earnings came in at $0.67 per share, down six cents from the year before.

Note that these numbers are not necessarily very indicative, as corporate cost allocation of the new entity was not yet factored in. This could explain the softer earnings guidance for the third quarter. While revenues were seen at a midpoint of $520 million, marking solid sequential revenue growth, adjusted earnings were down to $0.57 per share, plus or minus three cents.

Early in November, Ralliant reported flattish third-quarter sales at $529 million, which is flattish year-over-year. GAAP operating profits came in at $52 million, yet GAAP earnings fell to just $0.35 per share as the business incurred interest costs and costs as a standalone entity. Adjusted earnings came in at $0.60 per share, and while much of this adjustment related to amortization charges, stock-based compensation expenses were a big item as well. So early after the separation, it is uncertain whether this is structural or related to the separation.

And Now?

With 113 million shares trading around the $50 mark again, the market value stands at $5.65 billion. This excludes an $886 million net debt load, for a $6.5 billion enterprise valuation.

The company provides a solid outlook for the fourth quarter, seeing sales up to $535-$550 million, with adjusted earnings seen at a midpoint of $0.65 per share. This values the business at just over 3 times sales, a mid-teens EBITDA multiple, and, based on annualised adjusted earnings numbers, around 20 times earnings. However, the fourth quarter tends to be a bit stronger on a seasonal basis.

Frankly, the business is posting mixed results here, with the sensors & safety business showing real sales and margin momentum, offset by big declines at test & measurement, which is only really breaking even here. This also reveals the real potential here, and as part of the self-help, the company aims to deliver on another $9-$11 million in cost savings by the end of 2026, this being equal to nine cents per share on a pre-tax basis.

Based on the outlook, full-year sales might trend around $2.2 billion, although that is upbeat, as there is a seasonal impact to the business, with the final quarter being the strongest, and might easily grow further if the test & measurement segment recovers.

Moreover, 20% EBITDA margins look high but offer real upside as well, as every point improvement in margins could boost pre-tax earnings by $0.20 per share. That is a huge potential driver compared to $2.50 per share earnings power now, leaving a real roadmap for the business to post earnings at $3-$4 per share in the coming years. This would reveal real upside, but this requires real execution here, this being a big if.

What Now?

Shares have recovered from the lows, and now trade at par compared to the levels seen in the summer upon the business separation. The reality is that Ralliant is quite an interesting business.

Being well positioned, the business sees stable results now, driven by softness in the test & measurement business, which, if solved, could easily deliver on mid-single-digit comparable growth. Combined with margin gains, this could be a fascinating proposition.

That requires some real execution, but the situation is interesting enough to keep a close eye on the shares here, with an intention to revisit the thesis in 2026.

data.title: Macro View: Equity Income Strategies Like JEPI And SCHD Are Failing, Think Differently To Manage Risk
data.description:
I'm now in my 4th year writing on this platform, nearing 700 articles and more than 2,400 responses to comments. That's enough data to say with confidence that the topic that prompts the most spirited (careful choice of words there) reaction from this audience is what I'm writing about here. Equity Income investing. As I define it, this includes, but is not limited to:

  • Covered call ETFs
  • Dividend-driven ETFs
  • Some closed-end funds
  • Stocks with above-average yields

When I started publishing here, I was no stranger to writing about investing, having done it since my first letter to my then-advisory clients back in 1998. I still have the hard copy of what we called the "Emerald Educator." That was a very long time ago.

Given the era we were in 27 years ago, writing about the then-current dot-com-driven growth climate was just one aspect of market chatter, though it took most of the air in the room, as it does now. Here's what things looked like back then, as the bubble built to its crescendo in early 2000 for the then-brand-new Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ).

QQQ had the distinction of moving 75% during this period...in BOTH directions! First up, then down. That's when newer investors came to realize how the math of loss works. If you lose 75%, that means you lose 75% off the top, not 75% of your personal profit. If that sounds amateurish, given the simple math, back then it wasn't. Because we were not smothered in investment education, most people were just starting to even figure out what the S&P 500 was. QQQ excited them, drew them in, and ultimately spit them out.

That shock was so harsh, it reached the point where many well-heeled investors swore they'd never invest in the stock market again. They felt tricked. 2008 occurred less than a decade later, and it didn't help matters. Especially since many had just gained back the courage to re-enter the market around 2006-2007.

The other important aspects of that chart above, to set the stage to talk about today's market, are interest rates and a dividend growth mutual fund from Vanguard. The US 10-year Treasury yield had drifted higher during that time but then abruptly started falling as the economy weakened and the stock market fell. Well, not the whole stock market.

Dividend growth funds like the one shown continued to fly. That was because for a while into 2000, dividend growth and dividend investing in general were still moving nicely. Falling bond rates were one contributor, as was the ability of some non-tech sectors (consumer staples in particular) to have a delayed reaction to the QQQ-focused mess.

But by 2001, and through 2003, it all came falling down. QQQ was the leader, and everything else followed. Equity income strategies, such as those pictured below, hung in for a little while, then did what equity income strategies often do: they joined the high-correlation party, falling pretty much in line with the broad stock market. Because in all the years I've managed investment risk for a living, one of the best lessons I learned (the hard way!) is that the stock market ceases to be "broad" and "diversified" when the dung hits the fan.

And it doesn't matter what the reason is, since most market declines occur because a long-simmering issue finally gets noticed by enough big money to force prices down. Or, margin and leverage get crushed, and forced selling ensues.

It's not different this time: risk is still high, regardless of what ETF marketing departments lead us to believe

I've been writing about ETFs almost as long as there have been ETFs, and mutual funds prior to that. The first ETF (SPY) debuted in 1993; I started penning my thoughts formally in 1998. I've seen the evolution. It has taught me a lot of lessons. Enough so that along with Avoid Big Loss ("ABL") as my first investing rule, another phrase at the top of my priority list as an investor is "stay humble at all times." The markets know more than we do.

Thus, the goal for me is never to try to be "smarter" than the market, simply to listen to its story via charts and by recognizing when past trends are starting to reappear. That's happening right now, and the market is speaking loudly. But the marketing departments at fund companies, as well as mainstream financial media and social media, have us believing that the thrill is still there to chase the dream.

For some, it is the dream of higher returns. For others, it is focused on reliable, steady income, where the losses in principal will be made up in a reasonable amount of time. I cannot ever say that such a view won't be proven accurate. But I can do this, and often do: deliver the message, loud and clear, that risk is extraordinarily high for equity income investors.

For now, the risks I'll discuss below are not producing a lot of discomfort, because the headlines have people feeling like the stock market and equity income investing are as healthy as they have always been. I disagree. Humbly.

And as was the case back around the turn of the century, the market let a lot of things go. Part of that was the sheer force of investor capital rushing in to get their "fair share" of the action. A liquidity-fueled rally that became an obsession and, ultimately, a bubble.

Some time after that, during my investment advisory years, I took the classic "Cycle of Market Emotions" and applied Emojis to it (first published it when I wrote for Forbes.com). It has made the rounds of the years, and I think it merits another checkup here in late 2025.

Frankly, it doesn't matter where we are on this spectrum, only what the market thinks in aggregate. But if I were the only vote, I'd have us just to the right of Complacency. If you have your own thoughts, please tell me in the comments. This might be worth returning to as the focus of an article soon.

Risks to equity income investors, which are already showing up in performance

Let's start with the data, and then I can explain why I think as I do. And what I'm doing about it with my money, as well as how I'm conveying action items to my investing group subscribers at Sungarden Investors Club here at Seeking Alpha.

Takeaways from this busy table above, which shows 3-year trailing, annualized returns and standard deviation for a set of equity income proxies, and one of my own creations (the main one, in fact).

  • T-bills have done what they always do - manage risk on a nominal basis. But during the past 3 years, it has been a prime time for that type of investment. The writing is on the wall for more Fed rate cuts, and that directly reduces T-bill rates, as they are the closest thing in Treasury security land to the Fed Funds rate. In the chart below, I can't help noticing that T-bill rates are poised to nosedive just as they did in 2008 and in 2000. That's not just a coincidence.

  • Bonds have recovered after a terrible 2022, which is nearly gone from the 3-year figures above. That's allowed them to at least show reasonable 3-year returns after a historically poor period.
  • Now to the equity income proxies. I used what might be the 2 most popular around here, JP Morgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). There are many others that follow the same pattern.

These 1-year rolling returns are a signal. The stock market is down to its last big trade: AI stocks. And if that fades away (it will; I just do not know when), I am doubtful that, as in some past cases, the "average stock" can pick up for that. The average stock is already in the dumper, so to speak. And that includes higher-yield stocks.

This shows the erosion over the past 3 years of non-AI stocks quite well. The gap keeps getting wider. Remember, these are the same 500 stocks. But the orange line weights them by size, whereas the purple line weights them equally. Bull market for "stocks," eh? I think not.

While SCHD and other higher-yielding equity income funds have maintained its yield, that's because the stocks it tends to traffic in have not moved up with AI stocks. Similar stock price, similar dividend yield. Investors have earned a lot more with dividend growth ETFs, but they have the other problem: if the stocks fade, the income is not nearly enough to support reasonable positive returns for a while.

And as for the covered call ETFs, I'll write on them again soon with a deeper focus. But spoiler alert: they are usually only as good as the stock price returns associated with their underlying holdings. More on that soon. Cue the naysayers.

For now, note that JEPI's yield has been sliding for a while now. But this is not as noticeable as it would/will be when the market finally rolls over. That yield is simply not a cushion in anything other than when the stocks snap back up in price right after a steep drop. That's been the case every time since 2009. But given the rest of the gathering evidence here and below, I think it is riskier now than ever to rely on that bailing out equity income investors going forward.

And the older I get, the less I am willing to wait for the old "it will eventually come back" mantra. Especially with so many other ways to manage risk and generate return. Today's markets, to me, are more about engineering return and managing risk. Tightly.

What are the macro risks producing this view?

  1. The great thing about the Seeking Alpha community is that it is heavily populated with investors who want to learn from each other but with a longer-term focus. That's not the case in many corners of the investing world. Attention spans are micro-sized compared to just a few years ago. Everything is a buzzword, like "dividends," "buy the dip" and "cash-secured puts." I could go on, but the point is, younger retail investors are likely to continue imposing their will. And that takes a big portion of the usual demand away from equity income strategies.
  2. Regardless of that, the fact is that "indifferent" investors control the markets now. Algorithms and indexing combine (or shall I say conspire) to make it so that the value of our wealth is tossed around much more by what the bots say, not the fundamentals. The latter is still important, but it can be overwhelmed at any time. Ask small-cap and value investors about that, given their lower valuations. But lack of inertia, beyond momentary rallies.
  3. The stock market IS the economy, much more so than I can ever recall. The public is too reliant on stocks, and the number of stocks they are relying on is too few. The top-heavy nature of "the market" continues to be a risk.

4. Short-term bond yields may come down, but long-term yields can't come down enough to resolve some consumer debt mess. This cover from this week summarizes it. It has been a very long, arduous debt mountain built up.

The stock market will ultimately be the collateral damage, as liquidity reverses and stocks are used as a source of funds. And debt repayment. In particular, the risk I see is that this coincides with the realization that equity income strategies are just not getting it done like they used to. And they will be a main source of capital.

What I'm doing about it

As noted at the start of this article, I'm nearly 700 articles into this. So rehashing in detail what I've been doing with my own capital in great detail would be not only redundant but somewhat irrelevant. You see, I'm not one of those analysts who is doing this so that people will follow me pick by pick. Because to me it is not about picks but process. Because I don't know your financial situation, goals, resources, and risk attitude.

So I try to convey my own perspective, particularly on risk. For readers to learn from, discuss, debate, ask questions, and then do their own thing. My views are my views. And my intellectual property is my IP.

That's why I'll conclude by showing in slightly more detail the one item in that 3-year annualized return/standard deviation chart near the top of this article. Because it was my effort, starting with my Substack site at the beginning of 2022, to boil down my bottom-line approach to investing and any timeliness factors involved to a single number. I discussed it on the Seeking Alpha Investing Experts podcast earlier this year. Quoting myself from the transcript of that episode:

So when I'm looking at today's market and I'm looking at the charts, I'm looking first not for how much money I can make. I am looking for how much risk is attached to my pursuit of profits in this security. And that's really what the ROAR score is about. So, for example, going back to the beginning of 2022, I started kind of formally putting this out. And this is just my chart read and a bunch of other indicators, sentiment, fundamental, quantitative, the whole thing, thirty some odd years of doing this, all boiling down to a single number.

Like I said, it can be as simple or complex as you like. So, for instance, as we sit here today, once a week, I update this for subscribers. The ROAR score is at 40. What does that mean? Well, very simply, my market view specific to the S&P 500 is that if the only two investment options that I have are the S&P 500, the (SPY) ETF, and one to three month T bills. (BIL) is the symbol. It's a very simple paradigm.

The bottom line is this: I do not look very closely at the type of return I earn, only that I earn it. Dividend investing is not a stranger to me, nor is covered call writing. I used to manage an active mutual fund that included both.

And I started running it in August of 2008, which allowed me to be thrown to the fire when the S&P 500 fell by 55% the following 7 months. I knew then, as I do now, that "equity income" strategies are as vulnerable to market traps as anything else. So a while back, I de-emphasized income investing for myself and committed to a total return approach.

What followed in early 2022, when bond prices were poised to dive to an extent not seen since the 1980s and take equities with them, was my creation of the ROAR Score I link to above. ROAR stands for Reward Opportunity And Risk.

In other words: we all can find some "pick" to try to make a profit. But few in today's Wall Street focus on how much risk we take on to pursue that profit. ROAR has become the center of my investment life.

It is simply a number I calculate, formally each Tuesday but continuously in between, which can be simplified as follows: "if my only 2 choices are S&P 500 (SPY) and T-bills (BIL), how much of every $100 I have invested do I want to have in SPY? In other words, with a risk-free (virtually) alternative, there are times when I accept a lot of that as a shelter from market risk, and other times I am willing to take on more risk...because I think I'll be rewarded for it over the next many weeks and months.

This is a tactical measure, and while I run some of my own money strictly in that SPY-BIL, 2-ETF allocation, it is really more of a metaphor. For reward and risk, and balancing the two simultaneously. Or as I like to call it, "playing offense and defense at the same time."

That earlier chart (returns and standard deviation) shows that, in whatever form an individual investor wants to express it, playing offense and defense at the same time has been a very competitive and viable way to approach modern markets. I think that will only get stronger as the AI trade plays itself out.

Even as simply as I have been doing as depicted in that orange circle below (7.8% return with just under 3.0% standard deviation), that very constrained, weekly scoring system that produces one data point for me: how much of this account should be in SPY, the rest in BIL? It is based on my chart work going back about 30 years professionally and 40 years including my teen and college years. But the bottom line for me is it keeps me from falling in love with marketing pitches about "equity income."

Final thoughts for now

I'll write more on this soon, since I've just about finalized a way to automate the process of calculating the ROAR Score, not only for this one scenario, but for many ETFs and stocks. But the takeaway is this: offense and defense at the same time only works if the defense is really defense.

Stocks are stocks, and the concept of "my stocks will hold their value in a deep decline" is a major risk, now more than ever. And dividend income is effectively giving us our own money back. Sure, it is taken from corporate profits, but the stocks can still plummet. I just don't think it works as cleanly as it has historically.

And as for covered call writing, it's a great strategy, but by itself it is just a different type of offense, swapping income for price gains, if any. It is not a defensive mechanism.

I aim to write more on the detailed topics here. And I look forward to suggestions on what to cover in that deeper dive on risk management, the slide into mediocrity or worse for classic equity income approaches, and the ROAR approach to the markets we live in. Because they are VERY different from the ones many of us grew up with.

data.title: INDA: Lagging Other EMs, Why 2026 Could Be Its Year
data.description:
It's time to discuss a laggard in the emerging market space, namely the iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA). Although EMs such as Brazil, China, and South Africa have delivered staggering returns year-to-date, INDA ETF has traded flat, gaining merely ~3.21%. Various macro, political, and market-specific factors have justified INDA ETF's sluggish performance; however, I believe investors have overreacted to bad news. Moreover, I firmly believe India's economy and stock market could present outlying returns over the next decade, prompting me to do a bullish write-up of a tracker ETF such as INDA.

Without further ado, here are a few reasons why I'm positive about the iShares MSCI India ETF's prospects.

Dislocation Versus Other EMs

As mentioned in the introduction, we've seen other emerging market ETFs surge year-to-date. The likes of iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (EWZ), has gained by ~51%, iShares MSCI South Africa ETF (EZA) has gained by ~63%, and iShares MSCI China ETF (MCHI) ~35%.

The iShares MSCI India ETF's modest year-to-date gains, places its cross sectional z-score at -0.51, suggesting the ETF is cheap versus its historical average (regressed since January 2015). It should be noted that the iShares MSCI China ETF is also trading cheap with a z-score of -1.095. That being said, a regime change has occurred in recent years, whereby the sustainability of the Chinese growth story experienced from the 1990s until the late-2010s has been called into question. Moreover, as illustrated in the previous diagram, China has delivered strong year-to-date returns; I believe there's a case for India being a more pronounced laggard.

India's Economic & Market Variables

Real Economy

Figures 3 and 4 contain economic indicators that I extracted from various sources. Some are long-term structural indicators, whereas others are cyclical indicators. A critical consideration relates to the degree to which the real economy may influence the stock market, as there can be a disconnect. For example we might raise the question: Is GDP Growth and Market Performance Correlated? If you over-simplify it by merely looking at contemporaneous correlation, then no. However, markets to price implied GDP growth – it's all about correlation the correct periodicity. Moreover, index composition matters – certain indices possess stocks correlated to foreign economies (many of the companies focus on exports or foreign operations). However, in India's case, much of the index focuses on local banking and consumer goods.

Figure 3 illustrates a few long-run variables. In general, most sources forecast India's GDP growth to hit solid ground in the years ahead. As a single reference, EIU forecasts growth of 5.4% per year until 2050, which is evidently quite high, given that growth of 2% and above is generally considered robust. Furthermore, I enjoy the fact that India maintains a minor current account-to-GDP deficit, deficits of 4% and more would be cause for concern. In addition, India's debt load is relatively deflated versus developed market nations such as the U.S (118%+ gross) and Japan (235%+ gross). In my opinion, figures above 90% of GDP would result in revision, but with interest rates recently setting lower, I believe borrowing capacity will remain telling for the foreseeable future, especially with GDP growth remaining at high levels.

Variable Value
Annual Real GDP Growth Forecast 5.4% (2024-50)
Government Debt/GDP 81.92% (2024)
Unemployment Rate 5.2%
Current Account-to-GDP -0.6%

Sources: Trading Economics - Figure 3

Short-run variables have gathered pace as consumer confidence has risen steadily to 96.9 points from a low in January of 93.7 points. Furthermore, the housing index, which is a leading indicator to financial markets holds firm at 122 points, accompanied by a 100 basis point year-to-date decrease in interest rates and a 520 basis point decrease in inflation. These indicators signal a cyclical bottom; usually, a sharp decrease in inflation to near negative would be alarming (it shows weak demand). However, strong sentiment and robust GDP growth trump such worries.

Variable Value
Consumer Confidence 96.9
Housing Index 122
Quarterly-Reported Annual GDP Growth 8.2%
Interest Rates 5.5%
Inflation 0.25%

Sources: Trading Economics, Worldgovernmentbonds.com - Figure 4

In addition to the above-mentioned indicators, a number of qualitative factors deserve attention.

Qualitative Variables.

  • Central Bank Policies – India's Rupee has traded more freely since RBI Governor Sanjay Malhotra took office a year ago. Higher interim volatility might spook some investors. However, overall a more flexible regime allows for pre-emptive action to global shocks; it could assist India's economy with divergence from major developed markets.
  • International Trade – International trade is of particular concern to many, especially as India has been a prime target of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff agenda. India maintains bilateral relations with the west and the east. The west has attempted to manipulate its ties with Russia and China. However, despite short-run frictions, I see India being in a relatively sound position for the longer term; it has plenty of chips on the table, its cards are strong, and President Trump's trade war will likely be a regime-specific obstacle for global trade.

Market Premiums

If we look at India's market itself, the stock market possessed a risk premium of 7.26% going into 2025. For comparison, the U.S. presented a lower risk premium at the time (around 5.13%). India had a country-risk premium of roughly 2.93% going into 2025, which probably explained its higher ERP. That being said, I would take the 7.26% any day of the week, given India's economic outlook.

Metric Value
Equity Risk Premium (January, 2025) 7.26%
Country Risk Premium (January, 2025) 2.93%
5-Year CDS (Current) 87.67 Bps
Yield Spread ~250 bps

Source: NYU, Worldgovernmentbonds.com - Figure 5

Trivially, the aforementioned risk premiums have evolved throughout the year, meaning a cyclical view would require an updated set of data points. Cyclically, I'd cite the fact that India's CDS has expanded slightly to ~87.67 basis points from 84.04 in January. That being said, it's a small friction, which could most probably be explained by market microstructures as opposed to higher cyclical risk. I think the most telling factors to look at for a cyclical grasp would be the recent evolution of macro variables, especially interest rates and real growth.

Sectoral Support

Localised operations span a significant portion of India's stock market. Unlike China, India didn't ride the manufacturing/export wave during its recent redevelopment period. A such, I'm quite focused on India's local GDP growth, consumer sentiment, labour market development, and so forth. Figure 7 illustrates the high representation of banking, which I enjoy seeing as emerging market banking has the ability to tap into high lending rates while maintaining relatively low funding rates. I see the recent decline in interest rates as a net positive (it could reduce bank funding rates), especially as the yield curve remains sloped upward (lending rates aught to remain elevated). Furthermore, I find the ETF's exposure to consumer goods exciting (~18%), given 1) India's large consumer base, and 2) recent improvements in consumer sentiment. Lastly, I think lower interest rates paired with ongoing momentum in the economy will drive industrial goods and energy demand higher, allowing and additional ~18% of the ETF to benefit from cyclical demand-side factors.

Figure 8 provides an illustration of INDA ETF's top holdings. As shown by the table, the ETF hosts around 170 issuers, with non exceeding 10% of the book, suggesting concentration risk and idiosyncratic influence by single securities remains low. Holdings and position sizes are subject to change. For the time being, stocks such as HDFC Bank, Reliance Industries, ICICI Bank, Bharti Airtel, Infosys, Mahindra and Mahindra Ltd, Axis Bank, Bajaj Finance, and Tata remain front and centre.

Valuations Could Be Better; However, FWD Growth Is What I'm After

INDA ETF's valuation multiples aren't exactly mouthwatering, if observed at headline level. I mean, the ETF records a portfolio P/E Ratio of 25.97 and a price-to-book ratio of 3.89, which, in my view don't exactly signal deep value. That being said, I want to highlight two matters. Firstly, the portfolio is supported by robust underlying growth (as discussed earlier). Second, INDA ETF has a beta coefficient of merely 0.37, which suggests low covariance risk. Thus, after considering these variables, I think it's worth considering that INDA ETF's multiples won't be low, by design.

ETF-Specific Metrics
